moduleSecurity.approvals.closing: Reviewing and revoking unused or unlimited approvals periodically, using a block explorer's token approval checker, closes off access you may have forgotten you granted.
moduleSecurity.approvals.p1: To let a dApp move your tokens on your behalf (to swap or stake them, for example), you grant it a token approval, a permission recorded on-chain, separate from any individual transaction.
moduleSecurity.approvals.p2: Many interfaces default to requesting an unlimited approval, permission to move any amount, indefinitely, rather than just the amount needed for that transaction. That convenience is exactly what a malicious approval abuses.
moduleSecurity.approvals.title: Wallet Approvals & Permissions
moduleSecurity.audits.closing: A published audit is a genuinely good sign, but "audited" is a data point to weigh, not a guarantee to rely on completely.
moduleSecurity.audits.covers.text: The specific version of the code that was submitted for review, checked against known bug classes and the protocol's own stated logic.
moduleSecurity.audits.covers.title: What an audit typically covers
moduleSecurity.audits.doesntCover.text: That the deployed code matches what was audited, that every possible interaction was considered, or that the protocol's economic design itself is sound.
moduleSecurity.audits.doesntCover.title: What an audit doesn't guarantee
moduleSecurity.audits.p1: An audit is a time-boxed, expert review of a contract's code, looking for the kinds of bugs covered in the previous section, and others specific to the protocol's design.
moduleSecurity.audits.p2: An audit reduces risk, it doesn't eliminate it. It's a review of the code at a specific point in time, by people working within a limited timeframe, not a mathematical proof that no bug exists.
moduleSecurity.audits.title: What Audits Do (and Don't) Guarantee
moduleSecurity.exploits.closing: None of these require the attacker to break cryptography, they require the contract's logic to have a gap. This is exactly what an audit is meant to catch.
moduleSecurity.exploits.li1: Reentrancy. A contract sends funds out before updating its own internal balance, letting the same call re-enter and withdraw again before the first withdrawal is even recorded.
moduleSecurity.exploits.li2: Access control gaps. A function meant to be restricted (minting tokens, changing an admin) is missing the permission check that should have restricted it.
moduleSecurity.exploits.li3: Oracle manipulation. A contract that prices an asset using a manipulable source can be tricked into acting on a temporarily distorted price, often using a flash loan to fund the distortion.
moduleSecurity.exploits.p1: Most smart contract exploits don't "break" anything in a traditional hacking sense. The attacker interacts with the contract exactly as its code allows, just in a sequence or combination its developers didn't anticipate.
moduleSecurity.exploits.p2: A few categories of bugs show up repeatedly across incidents. Understanding what they are, at a conceptual level, helps you understand why audits and defensive coding patterns exist.
moduleSecurity.exploits.title: Smart Contract Exploits
moduleSecurity.glossary.def1: An independent review of a smart contract's code, looking for bugs and vulnerabilities.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def2: An attack that takes advantage of a specific bug or design flaw to steal or misdirect funds.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def3: A scam where a project's creators withdraw all liquidity or funds, abandoning users.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def4: A common smart contract bug where a function is re-entered before its state is updated.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def5: Operational security, the personal habits that reduce your exposure to attacks.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def6: An attempt to trick someone into revealing sensitive information or signing a malicious transaction, usually by impersonating something trusted.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def7: An on-chain permission letting a contract move a specified (or unlimited) amount of your tokens on your behalf.
moduleSecurity.glossary.def8: An attack that distorts the price feed a contract relies on, tricking it into acting on an inaccurate value.
moduleSecurity.glossary.title: Glossary
moduleSecurity.hero.description: Most losses in this space are preventable. This module explains the habits and red flags that matter most.
moduleSecurity.hero.stat1.label: Difficulty
moduleSecurity.hero.stat2.label: Duration
moduleSecurity.hero.stat3.label: Format
moduleSecurity.hero.stat4.label: Sections
moduleSecurity.hero.title: Security
moduleSecurity.ifCompromised.li1: Move remaining funds immediately. Send anything still safe to a fresh wallet whose keys have never touched the compromised device.
moduleSecurity.ifCompromised.li2: Revoke approvals from the old wallet. Use a token approval checker to cut off any permission the attacker's contract still holds.
moduleSecurity.ifCompromised.li3: Treat the device as compromised, not just the wallet. If malware or a fake app is the likely cause, a new wallet on the same device doesn't fix the root problem.
moduleSecurity.ifCompromised.li4: Be wary of "recovery" offers. Unsolicited help offering to recover stolen funds, for an upfront fee, is itself one of the most common follow-up scams.
moduleSecurity.ifCompromised.p1: If you suspect a wallet has been compromised, or that you signed something malicious, speed matters more than certainty.
moduleSecurity.ifCompromised.title: If You Get Compromised
moduleSecurity.introduction.p1: The overwhelming majority of losses in crypto come from a small set of repeated patterns: phishing, contract exploits, and avoidable operational mistakes, not exotic, unstoppable attacks.
moduleSecurity.introduction.p2: This lesson is written from a defender's perspective. It explains what these attacks look like and why they work, so you can recognize the pattern, not how to carry one out.
moduleSecurity.introduction.p3: By the end of this lesson you'll be able to recognize common phishing patterns, understand what a smart contract audit does and doesn't guarantee, spot the warning signs of a rug pull, and build the personal habits that remove most realistic risk.
moduleSecurity.introduction.title: Introduction
moduleSecurity.meta.title: Security — Nodra Academy
moduleSecurity.opsec.li1: Separate wallets by purpose. Keep a small "hot" wallet for everyday interactions and a hardware wallet for meaningful holdings, exactly the hot/cold split covered in Module 02.
moduleSecurity.opsec.li2: Never share your seed phrase. No legitimate wallet, exchange, or support channel will ever need it, anyone asking for it is attacking you.
moduleSecurity.opsec.li3: Verify URLs before connecting. Bookmark official sites you use regularly instead of searching for them each time, search ads are a common phishing vector.
moduleSecurity.opsec.li4: Read what you sign. A wallet's signature prompt describes what permission you're granting, treat a request you don't understand as a reason to stop, not to proceed.
moduleSecurity.opsec.li5: Be skeptical of urgency. "Act now or lose your funds" is a pressure tactic used by nearly every phishing attempt, a genuine issue rarely requires an instant, unverified reaction.
moduleSecurity.opsec.title: Personal Security Habits
moduleSecurity.phishing.card1.text: Near-identical copies of real dApp interfaces, often bought as top search ads, that connect your wallet and request a malicious signature.
moduleSecurity.phishing.card1.title: Fake Websites & Ads
moduleSecurity.phishing.card2.text: Someone posing as "support" in a Discord or Telegram, offering to help, then asking you to share a screen, a seed phrase, or click a "verification" link.
moduleSecurity.phishing.card2.title: Fake Support & DMs
moduleSecurity.phishing.card3.text: A transaction disguised as something routine (a mint, a claim) that actually requests broad, ongoing permission to move your tokens.
moduleSecurity.phishing.card3.title: Malicious Approvals
moduleSecurity.phishing.closing: The common thread across all three is the same: a request to sign, connect, or reveal something, wrapped in urgency. No legitimate support agent will ever ask for your seed phrase.
moduleSecurity.phishing.p1: Phishing is the single largest cause of loss in crypto, by a wide margin. It doesn't exploit a weakness in a blockchain or a wallet, it exploits trust, and a moment of urgency.
moduleSecurity.phishing.title: Phishing
moduleSecurity.quiz.intro: Check your understanding of this lesson before moving on. Incorrect answers point you straight back to the relevant section.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q1.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Phishing and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q1o1: A. A weakness in blockchain cryptography
moduleSecurity.quiz.q1o2: B. Trust and a moment of urgency
moduleSecurity.quiz.q1o3: C. A bug in the wallet's hardware
moduleSecurity.quiz.q1o4: D. A flaw only present in old wallets
moduleSecurity.quiz.q2: 2. Which of these should always be treated as a phishing attempt?
moduleSecurity.quiz.q2.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Phishing and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q2o1: A. A wallet update notification inside the wallet app itself
moduleSecurity.quiz.q2o2: B. Someone asking for your seed phrase to "verify" your account
moduleSecurity.quiz.q2o3: C. A block explorer showing your transaction history
moduleSecurity.quiz.q2o4: D. A hardware wallet asking you to confirm a transaction on its screen
moduleSecurity.quiz.q3: 3. What is reentrancy, at a conceptual level?
moduleSecurity.quiz.q3.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Smart Contract Exploits and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q3o1: A. A contract sending funds out before updating its own internal balance
moduleSecurity.quiz.q3o2: B. A wallet that lets you reuse the same password
moduleSecurity.quiz.q3o3: C. A type of phishing email sent more than once
moduleSecurity.quiz.q3o4: D. Two users sharing the same private key
moduleSecurity.quiz.q4: 4. What does a smart contract audit NOT guarantee?
moduleSecurity.quiz.q4.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review What Audits Do (and Don't) Guarantee and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q4o1: A. That the code was reviewed by someone other than its own developers
moduleSecurity.quiz.q4o2: B. That the deployed code perfectly matches what was audited, with no bug possible
moduleSecurity.quiz.q4o3: C. That known bug classes were checked for
moduleSecurity.quiz.q4o4: D. That the audit happened at a specific point in time
moduleSecurity.quiz.q5: 5. Which is a genuine rug pull warning sign?
moduleSecurity.quiz.q5.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Rug Pulls and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q5o1: A. Liquidity locked for a fixed period with no early withdrawal
moduleSecurity.quiz.q5o2: B. The contract owner retains the power to mint unlimited new tokens at will
moduleSecurity.quiz.q5o3: C. A published, dated audit report
moduleSecurity.quiz.q5o4: D. A vesting schedule on the team's own tokens
moduleSecurity.quiz.q6: 6. Why are unlimited token approvals risky?
moduleSecurity.quiz.q6.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Wallet Approvals & Permissions and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q6o1: A. They cost more gas than limited approvals
moduleSecurity.quiz.q6o2: B. They grant ongoing permission to move any amount, indefinitely, which a malicious contract can abuse
moduleSecurity.quiz.q6o3: C. They automatically expire after 24 hours
moduleSecurity.quiz.q6o4: D. They are required by every legitimate dApp
moduleSecurity.quiz.q7: 7. What's the most important first step if you suspect your wallet was compromised?
moduleSecurity.quiz.q7.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review If You Get Compromised and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q7o1: A. Wait a few days to see if anything happens
moduleSecurity.quiz.q7o2: B. Move remaining funds to a fresh wallet immediately
moduleSecurity.quiz.q7o3: C. Share your seed phrase with a support agent to investigate
moduleSecurity.quiz.q7o4: D. Accept an unsolicited recovery offer to get funds back
moduleSecurity.quiz.q8: 8. What is the most reliable sign that a message or request is malicious?
moduleSecurity.quiz.q8.feedbackIncorrect: Not quite, review Phishing and try again.
moduleSecurity.quiz.q8o1: A. It is written in poor grammar
moduleSecurity.quiz.q8o2: B. It creates urgency and asks you to sign, connect, or reveal something
moduleSecurity.quiz.q8o3: C. It comes from an official-looking domain
moduleSecurity.quiz.q8o4: D. It mentions a specific token you hold
moduleSecurity.quiz.title: Quiz
moduleSecurity.rugPulls.intro: A rug pull is a scam where a project's creators withdraw liquidity or funds and disappear, rather than a technical exploit of someone else's code. A few structural warning signs tend to precede one.
moduleSecurity.rugPulls.li1: Unlocked liquidity. If the team can withdraw the pool's liquidity at any time, with no lock or time delay, nothing stops them from doing so.
moduleSecurity.rugPulls.li2: Unlimited minting power. A function that lets the owner create new tokens at will can be used to dilute or dump on holders without warning.
moduleSecurity.rugPulls.li3: Anonymous team, no lock-up. Anonymity alone isn't disqualifying, but combined with no vesting schedule on team tokens, it removes any cost to walking away.
moduleSecurity.rugPulls.li4: Retained owner privileges. A contract owner that keeps the ability to pause trading, blacklist addresses, or change fees after launch holds a lot of leverage over holders.
moduleSecurity.rugPulls.title: Rug Pulls
moduleSecurity.summary.p1: Most losses in crypto trace back to phishing, contract logic gaps, or avoidable operational habits, not unstoppable attacks. Recognizing the pattern is most of the defense.
moduleSecurity.summary.p2: An audit and a locked liquidity pool are good signs, not guarantees. The habits that remove the most realistic risk are simple: separate wallets by purpose, never share a seed phrase, read what you sign, and treat urgency as a reason to slow down.
moduleSecurity.summary.title: Summary
moduleSecurity.toc.title: Contents
